title: Lesson Quiz: Needs, Wants, and Financial Priorities
description: Assessment of the concept that Distinguishing spending priorities and value-based decision-making.

questions.prompt: Which example is most likely a financial need?

choices.feedback: Correct. A required fee is a stronger example of a financial need than an optional purchase.
A video game is usually a want, not a need.
Optional snacks are usually wants rather than financial needs.
A fashion accessory is usually a want unless there is a clear required need.

questions.prompt: When money is limited, priorities help a person decide what should be paid first.

choices.feedback: Priorities do help a person choose which expenses should come first.
Correct. Priorities help rank expenses when not every option can be paid at once.

questions.prompt: Why should needs usually come before wants in financial planning?

choices.text: Because wants always cost more than needs
choices.feedback: A want does not always cost more than a need.

choices.text: Because needs often support responsibilities, safety, or required costs
choices.feedback: This is a strong reason, but the correct answer in this set most directly connects priorities to consequences.

choices.text: Because wants never have any value
choices.feedback: Wants can still have value, but they are not always the first priority.

choices.text: Because paying needs first helps avoid bigger problems later
choices.feedback: Correct. Paying needs first often prevents future problems and protects financial stability.

questions.prompt: A student has enough money for either a bus pass or a concert ticket, but not both. Which is the strongest financial priority?

choices.text: The concert ticket, because it is more exciting
choices.feedback: Excitement does not automatically make something the stronger financial priority.

choices.text: The bus pass, because it supports transportation needs
choices.feedback: This is a strong answer, but the correct choice in this set more directly connects the bus pass to priority-based decision-making.

choices.text: The bus pass, because a required transportation expense should come before an optional want
choices.feedback: Correct. The bus pass is the stronger financial priority because it supports a need.

choices.text: Neither option, because priorities do not matter
choices.feedback: Priorities do matter when a person must choose between limited options.

questions.prompt: A want can still be purchased later after stronger financial priorities are handled.

choices.feedback: Correct. Wants may still matter, but they usually come after stronger priorities are handled.
Wants can still be part of spending decisions, but usually after stronger priorities are covered.
